pages.full_text: harbour that was built by the British, starting on June 8th 1944. We saw 3 films about the landings. The Americans trying to scale the cliffs at Omaha used ladders from the London fire department. [last line partially cut off] on each beach in the first day
pages.notes: Page contains a glued-in postcard/map of Operation Overlord D-Day 6 June 1944 (JOUR J) showing the five landing beaches: Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, Sword, with associated military units and commanders. The map text is printed not handwritten. The handwritten text below the postcard continues mid-sentence from a previous page and is cut off at the bottom. The postcard also references Pegasus Bridge and Caen.

pages.full_text:
ranged between 300 & 900, except on Bloody Omaha where the US lost 2000. The harbour was sheltered from the s.w. by cliffs sticking out into the sea. Ready made concrete boxes were towed over from England & the "Leviathan" gulped up water & sand in the harbour to make them sink. The actual road ways were started by engineering companies all over GB in 1942.

[hand-drawn diagram of harbour with labels: lorries loaded with crates, empty lorries + wounded, lorries loaded with crates]

The harbour worked from June 8th [old ships
pages.route_sketch_description: Hand-drawn diagram of the Mulberry harbour showing a curved breakwater of rectangular blocks (concrete caissons), a central pier/jetty structure, and arrows/labels indicating: 'lorries loaded with crates' (on left), 'empty lorries + wounded' (centre, pointing inward), 'lorries loaded with crates' (on right). The diagram illustrates the layout and traffic flow of the wartime artificial harbour.
pages.notes: Text is written in pink/magenta ink throughout this page, contrasting with the blue ink on the left page — likely a different pen or written at a different time. The page contains a glued-in photo at the bottom showing a section of the Bayeux Tapestry with Latin text 'VBI HAROLD SACRAMENTVM FECIT: HIC HAROL[D]: VVILLELMO DVCI:-' visible. The word 'Leviathan' refers to a ship or piece of equipment used in construction of the Mulberry harbour at Arromanches. The last line is cut off — text continues onto next page. The handwritten page number 48 is in the top right corner.
